Governing the Stack: Regulatory Jurisdiction in Multi-Layer AI Systems
Maps jurisdictional gaps across AI model layers, fine-tuning, APIs, and deployment contexts, proposing a tiered liability framework.

## Abstract

As AI systems grow architecturally complex, existing regulatory frameworks struggle to assign meaningful accountability. A model trained by one entity, fine-tuned by a second, deployed via API by a third, and integrated into a consumer product by a fourth creates a liability chain that no current doctrine handles cleanly.

This paper maps the jurisdictional gaps that emerge across each layer of modern AI system architecture and proposes a tiered liability framework calibrated to each party's degree of control and foreseeability of harm.

## The Multi-Layer Problem

Contemporary AI deployment rarely involves a single actor. The "stack" at minimum includes: a foundation model developer, an API or platform provider, a fine-tuning or customization layer, and a downstream deployer facing end users. Each layer exercises meaningful discretion; none has complete visibility into what the others do.

Regulatory frameworks written for simpler products — where a manufacturer produces a finished good and a retailer sells it — do not translate well to this architecture. The question of who bears responsibility when an AI system causes harm is genuinely unsettled, and courts and agencies are only beginning to develop doctrine adequate to the problem.

## A Tiered Liability Framework

This paper proposes assigning regulatory obligations in proportion to architectural control and informational advantage at each layer...

Intermediary Liability in the Age of Generative AI: Rethinking Platform Responsibility
Asks whether Section 230's third-party content framework can coherently extend to AI-generated outputs produced at a platform's direction.

## Abstract

Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act immunizes platforms from liability for third-party content. The statute was written for bulletin boards and early social networks, where users posted content and platforms hosted it. Generative AI inverts this dynamic: the platform produces content, at a user's direction, from its own systems.

This paper asks whether Section 230 immunity can coherently extend to AI-generated outputs, and what the answer implies for platform liability doctrine.

## The Third-Party Content Framework

Section 230(c)(1) provides that no provider of an interactive computer service shall be treated as the publisher or speaker of information provided by another content provider. Three elements matter here: the defendant must be a provider; the content must be provided by another; and the claim must treat the defendant as a publisher of that content.

Each element is stressed when applied to generative AI outputs...

*(Full paper continues.)*

Algorithmic Harm and the Limits of Disparate Impact Theory
Examines whether Title VII's disparate impact doctrine can bear the weight of automated decision-making in employment contexts.

## Abstract

Title VII's disparate impact doctrine was designed for a world of human decision-makers, where the causal chain between a facially neutral policy and a discriminatory outcome is relatively legible. Algorithmic hiring and screening tools introduce opacity, complexity, and statistical interdependencies that the doctrine was not built to handle.

This paper examines the structural limits of disparate impact theory as applied to automated employment decisions and asks whether the doctrine can be adapted — or whether a new framework is required.

## The Doctrinal Foundation

Griggs v. Duke Power Co. established that facially neutral employment practices with disparate racial effects violate Title VII absent business justification. The framework has three steps: the plaintiff must demonstrate a statistically significant disparate impact; the burden shifts to the employer to justify the practice as job-related and consistent with business necessity; and the plaintiff may still prevail by showing a less discriminatory alternative practice exists.

Each step presents distinct challenges when the "practice" is an algorithmic system whose internal workings are complex, proprietary, and often opaque even to the employer operating it.

## Where the Doctrine Strains

The first challenge is causation. Disparate impact requires plaintiffs to identify a specific employment practice causing the disparity. Where an algorithm integrates hundreds of variables and their interactions, isolating a causal mechanism is analytically difficult and practically burdensome...

*(Full paper continues.)*

The Federal Privacy Gap: Why Sectoral Regulation Has Failed and What Comes Next
A structural critique of the United States' sector-by-sector approach to data privacy and a framework for comprehensive federal legislation.

## Abstract

The United States has governed data privacy through a patchwork of sector-specific statutes: HIPAA for health data, FERPA for education records, GLBA for financial information. This approach made sense when data stayed within sectoral boundaries. It does not make sense now.

This paper offers a structural critique of sectoral privacy regulation, examines why comprehensive federal legislation has failed to pass, and proposes a framework for what effective federal privacy law would need to contain.

## The Sectoral Model and Its Origins

American privacy law developed reactively. Each statute responded to a specific demonstrated harm in a defined context: HIPAA addressed insurance industry misuse of health records; COPPA responded to commercial collection of children's data online. The result is a body of law organized around industries rather than data types, harms, or individual rights.

This reactive, sector-by-sector approach has three structural weaknesses that become more severe as data flows across sectoral boundaries with increasing frequency...

*(Full paper continues.)*

Market Power in the Foundation Model Layer: Early Evidence and Regulatory Implications
Applies traditional antitrust analysis to the emerging market structure of large language model development and API access.

## Abstract

The foundation model layer of the AI stack exhibits early markers of natural monopoly dynamics: enormous fixed costs for training, high barriers to entry, and strong network effects from data accumulation and ecosystem lock-in. This paper applies traditional antitrust analysis to this emerging market structure and identifies the regulatory implications.

## Market Definition

Antitrust analysis begins with market definition — a threshold question that is surprisingly contested in the foundation model context. The relevant product market could be defined as frontier general-purpose language models, AI API access, or more broadly as AI-enabled software services. Each definition produces different market structure analyses and different assessments of competitive harm...

Transatlantic AI Governance: A Framework for U.S.–EU Regulatory Cooperation
Proposes a bilateral coordination mechanism to reduce compliance friction and establish shared standards across the Atlantic AI governance divide.

## Overview

The United States and European Union are developing AI governance frameworks on parallel tracks, with limited formal coordination and growing divergence in approach. The EU AI Act establishes a risk-tiered, pre-market approval model; the U.S. approach remains fragmented across agencies and sectors. Absent coordination, firms operating transatlantically face conflicting requirements, compliance costs multiply, and the resulting friction may disadvantage both blocs relative to less regulated competitors.

This proposal outlines a bilateral coordination mechanism — modeled loosely on the Privacy Shield successor framework and existing financial regulatory cooperation arrangements — designed to reduce compliance friction while preserving each jurisdiction's regulatory autonomy.

*(Full proposal continues.)*

Model Algorithmic Impact Assessment Standards for State Agencies
Draft standards for how state governments should evaluate automated decision systems before deployment in public services.

## Overview

State agencies increasingly rely on automated decision systems for consequential determinations: benefits eligibility, child welfare risk scoring, pretrial detention recommendations, and public safety resource allocation. Most of these deployments occur without structured evaluation of accuracy, fairness, or accountability.

This document proposes model Algorithmic Impact Assessment (AIA) standards for state agencies, drawing on existing frameworks from Canada, the EU, and the small number of U.S. jurisdictions that have adopted requirements.

A Federal AI Incident Reporting Framework
Drawing from aviation and nuclear safety models, a concrete proposal for mandatory AI incident disclosure requirements at the federal level.

## Executive Summary

Aviation and nuclear power are among the safest complex industries in the world. They became so not through perfect engineering but through systematic incident reporting: mandatory disclosure of failures, near-misses, and anomalies that allows regulators, operators, and the broader industry to learn from harm before it becomes catastrophic.

AI systems deployed at scale are causing measurable harms — discriminatory decisions, misinformation amplification, safety failures in autonomous contexts — with no comparable reporting infrastructure. This proposal outlines a mandatory federal AI incident reporting framework modeled on proven precedents.

## The Case for Mandatory Reporting

Voluntary incident reporting produces systematically incomplete data. Firms have reputational and legal incentives to minimize disclosure; without mandatory requirements, the incidents that surface are unrepresentative of the actual distribution of harms. The result is a feedback-poor environment in which regulators, researchers, and the public cannot accurately assess risk or identify systemic problems.

## Proposed Framework

**Scope.** Reporting obligations should apply to AI systems deployed in high-stakes domains: employment, credit, housing, healthcare, critical infrastructure, and public safety. General-purpose consumer applications should be subject to lighter reporting requirements tied to materiality thresholds.

**Trigger Events.** Reportable incidents should include: system outputs that cause documented physical, financial, or dignitary harm; significant failures in safety-critical applications; and anomalous system behaviors inconsistent with intended function...

*(Full proposal continues.)*

Procurement as Policy: How Federal Contracting Could Shape Responsible AI
A framework for using federal procurement power to establish baseline AI safety and accountability requirements before broader legislation passes.

## Executive Summary

The federal government spends over $700 billion annually on contracts. That purchasing power is a regulatory instrument of the first order — one that has historically been deployed to advance civil rights, labor standards, and environmental goals before Congress acted through legislation. This proposal applies the same logic to AI accountability.

Federal procurement requirements can establish meaningful baseline standards for AI systems used in government contexts, create market incentives that shape private-sector practice, and do so within existing executive authority — without waiting for comprehensive AI legislation that may not arrive for years.

*(Full proposal continues.)*

FTC v. Amazon: What the Complaint Reveals About Modern Monopolization Theory
An analysis of the FTC's Amazon complaint as a window into how enforcement agencies are attempting to modernize Sherman Act Section 2 doctrine.

## Overview

The FTC's complaint against Amazon, filed in September 2023, is more than an enforcement action against a specific firm. It is a statement of theory — an attempt by the agency to develop a Section 2 monopolization framework adequate to the economics of platform markets, where traditional price-based harm analysis systematically understates competitive harm.

This analysis reads the complaint as a doctrinal document and evaluates its prospects.

*(Full analysis continues.)*

Copyright and the Training Data Question: Where Courts Are Headed
Surveys pending litigation over AI training data and sketches the likely doctrinal paths courts will take to resolve the fair use question.

## Overview

Dozens of lawsuits challenging the use of copyrighted material to train AI systems are currently pending in federal courts. The central question — whether training on copyrighted works without license constitutes fair use — will shape the economics of AI development and the rights of creators for a generation.

This analysis surveys the pending litigation, identifies the key doctrinal fault lines, and sketches the most likely paths courts will take to resolution.

## The Fair Use Framework

Fair use analysis under 17 U.S.C. § 107 turns on four factors: the purpose and character of the use; the nature of the copyrighted work; the amount and substantiality of the portion used; and the effect on the potential market for the original. No factor is dispositive; courts weigh them together.

The training data cases present a genuinely novel application of the framework. The use is transformative in the sense that the output is not a copy of the input, but the input is consumed wholesale. The market effect is contested...

*(Full analysis continues.)*

Compelled Speech and Algorithmic Curation After Moody v. NetChoice
Examines whether must-carry and must-not-carry obligations on platforms constitute compelled speech under the First Amendment as clarified by Moody.

## Overview

Must-carry obligations — requirements that platforms host content they would otherwise remove — and must-not-carry obligations — prohibitions on removing content platforms would otherwise suppress — both implicate the First Amendment's compelled speech doctrine. *Moody v. NetChoice* clarified the framework without resolving its application to algorithmic curation.

This analysis examines what compelled speech doctrine, post-*Moody*, permits and prohibits in the platform regulation context.

Section 230 After NetChoice: What the Court Left Unsaid
A close reading of the 2024 NetChoice opinions, the doctrinal questions they resolved, and the larger questions they deliberately avoided.

## Overview

The Supreme Court's 2024 opinions in *Moody v. NetChoice* and *NetChoice v. Paxton* resolved certain questions about the First Amendment status of platform content moderation while carefully avoiding others. The Court vacated both lower court decisions on facial challenge grounds, holding that neither court had adequately analyzed the full range of applications of the challenged statutes.

What the Court did not do is equally significant. It declined to hold that platforms have an absolute First Amendment right to moderate as they choose; it declined to hold that states may freely mandate content neutrality; and it left open the precise scope of editorial discretion protection in the algorithmic context.

## What Was Decided

The most important holding is methodological: facial challenges to content moderation laws require courts to assess the laws' constitutionality across their full range of applications, not merely in the context of a platform's most prominent editorial choices. This significantly raises the burden on plaintiffs seeking to strike down such laws in their entirety.

## What Was Not Decided

The Court explicitly reserved several questions that will define platform First Amendment doctrine for the next decade...

*(Full analysis continues.)*

Loper Bright and the Future of FCC Rulemaking Authority
Traces the implications of the Court's Chevron overruling for the FCC's ability to promulgate broadband and platform regulations.

## Overview

*Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo* overruled *Chevron U.S.A. v. NRDC*, eliminating judicial deference to agency interpretations of ambiguous statutes. For the FCC, an agency whose regulatory authority rests on a 1996 statute that predates the internet as a mass phenomenon, the implications are significant.

This analysis traces the specific FCC rulemakings most vulnerable to post-*Loper Bright* challenge and assesses what remains of the Commission's broadband and platform regulatory authority.

*(Full analysis continues.)*

Why "AI Safety" and "AI Regulation" Are Not the Same Conversation
The conflation of technical safety research with policy regulation has muddied both fields. They share concerns but operate on different timescales, with different tools, for different audiences.

The term "AI safety" entered mainstream policy discourse carrying two distinct meanings that have never been cleanly separated. The first is technical: safety as an engineering property of AI systems, concerning alignment, robustness, interpretability, and the prevention of unintended behavior. The second is political: safety as a regulatory outcome, concerning what rules govern who may build AI systems, for what purposes, with what oversight.

These are not the same conversation. Conflating them — as policymakers, journalists, and advocates routinely do — produces bad analysis in both directions.

## The Technical Safety Project

AI safety as a technical discipline is concerned with a specific class of problems: how to build AI systems that reliably do what their designers intend, that do not behave badly in deployment contexts their designers did not anticipate, and that remain controllable as they become more capable. The relevant literature sits at the intersection of machine learning, decision theory, and formal verification.

This work operates on long timescales, involves deep technical uncertainty, and its primary audiences are researchers and engineers. It is not, in any direct sense, a policy project — though it has policy implications.

## The Regulatory Project

AI regulation as a policy discipline is concerned with a different class of problems: who bears legal responsibility when AI systems cause harm, what pre-deployment requirements are appropriate for high-risk applications, how antitrust and competition law applies to AI markets, and what institutional arrangements are adequate to oversee a rapidly developing technology sector.

These questions are urgent on shorter timescales, are addressed to legislators, regulators, and courts, and are tractable with existing legal and policy tools.

## Why the Conflation Matters

When the two projects are conflated, several bad things happen...

What Open Source Actually Means in the Context of AI Models
The word "open" is doing enormous work in AI policy debates. This essay tries to disaggregate the term and ask which version of openness actually matters for accountability and access.

"Open source AI" has become a politically significant phrase in a debate where its meaning is deeply contested. Advocates invoke it as a proxy for accountability, democratization, and resistance to concentrated control. Critics invoke it as a risk amplifier enabling misuse without clear benefit. Both sides are often arguing past each other because they are using the word "open" to mean different things.

This essay attempts to disaggregate the term and ask, with some precision, which versions of openness actually matter for the values most commonly invoked in the debate.

## What "Open" Can Mean

In the software context, "open source" has a technical definition: access to source code under a license permitting use, modification, and redistribution. Applied to AI models, the term has been stretched to cover a range of practices that differ materially from each other:

**Weights-open**: Release of trained model parameters, enabling local deployment and fine-tuning, but not necessarily reproduction of the training process.

**Training-data-open**: Disclosure of the data on which a model was trained, enabling analysis of what the model learned and from whom.

**Architecture-open**: Publication of model design, enabling others to train similar systems from scratch.

**Process-open**: Transparency about training methodology, safety evaluation, and deployment decisions.

These are distinct properties. A model can be weights-open without being training-data-open...

*(Full essay continues.)*

On the Poverty of "Move Fast and Fix It Later" as Governance Philosophy
A critique of the regulatory philosophy that treats market deployment as the primary mechanism for identifying harm, and the populations that approach systematically disadvantages.

There is a governance philosophy embedded in the phrase "move fast and break things" that survived the phrase's fall from grace. Its current form is softer: deploy first, identify harms through real-world feedback, then regulate. Call it move fast and fix it later.

This essay argues that this approach, while it has genuine virtues, functions as a distributional choice — one that systematically transfers the costs of technological risk onto populations with the least ability to absorb them, and that the field should be more willing to name it as such.

## The Steel-Man

The strongest version of the argument for permissive pre-deployment frameworks is empirical: we are generally bad at predicting the harms that new technologies will produce before deployment. Regulatory frameworks built on pre-deployment approval risk enshrining the predictions of early regulators, suppressing beneficial innovation, and freezing in place the market positions of incumbents who can absorb compliance costs.

There is real evidence for this. Regulatory processes for novel technologies have a mixed track record of accurately identifying risks in advance...

## The Distributional Problem

What the steel-man omits is the question of who bears the cost of learning...

(Full essay continues.)

Regulators and courts often aspire to write technology-neutral rules. The argument here is that this aspiration, while understandable, frequently produces worse outcomes than honest technology-specific reasoning.

Technology-neutral regulation has become something close to orthodoxy in legal and policy circles. The aspiration is understandable: rules that apply equally to all technologies avoid picking winners and losers, remain relevant as the technology landscape shifts, and resist capture by incumbents seeking favorable treatment.

The argument of this essay is that technology neutrality, pursued as a design principle, frequently produces worse outcomes than honest technology-specific reasoning — and that the field should be more willing to say so.

## The Case for Neutrality

The standard argument for technology-neutral rules runs as follows. Technology changes faster than law; rules written to the specifics of one technology become obsolete or arbitrary as the technology evolves. A rule written for videotape rental (VPPA, 1988) requires increasingly strained interpretation when applied to streaming. Better to write the rule at a level of generality that encompasses both.

There is also a political economy argument: technology-specific rules are more vulnerable to lobbying by the regulated firms, who have strong incentives to shape the rules that apply specifically to them.

## Where Neutrality Fails

The neutrality principle fails in three characteristic ways...

*(Full essay continues.)*

Technology-neutral regulation has become something close to orthodoxy in legal and policy circles. The aspiration is understandable: rules that apply equally to all technologies avoid picking winners and losers, remain relevant as the technology landscape shifts, and resist capture by incumbents seeking favorable treatment.

The argument of this essay is that technology neutrality, pursued as a design principle, frequently produces worse outcomes than honest technology-specific reasoning — and that the field should be more willing to say so.

The Case for Neutrality

The standard argument for technology-neutral rules runs as follows. Technology changes faster than law; rules written to the specifics of one technology become obsolete or arbitrary as the technology evolves. A rule written for videotape rental (VPPA, 1988) requires increasingly strained interpretation when applied to streaming. Better to write the rule at a level of generality that encompasses both.

There is also a political economy argument: technology-specific rules are more vulnerable to lobbying by the regulated firms, who have strong incentives to shape the rules that apply specifically to them.

Where Neutrality Fails

The neutrality principle fails in three characteristic ways…

Foundation Model Providers and the Regulatory Landscape: An Industry Overview
Maps the current regulatory exposure of major foundation model providers across jurisdictions, including the EU AI Act, U.S. executive orders, and pending state legislation.

## Overview

Foundation model providers now operate in a multi-jurisdictional regulatory environment that is actively evolving. The EU AI Act's provisions for general-purpose AI models became applicable in August 2025; U.S. federal activity remains fragmented but is intensifying; and a growing number of states have enacted or are considering legislation with direct implications for model developers.

This report maps the current regulatory exposure of major foundation model providers across the key jurisdictions, identifies the most significant near-term compliance requirements, and notes areas of ongoing uncertainty.

The FTC's Algorithmic Accountability Agenda: A Two-Year Review
A comprehensive review of FTC enforcement actions, policy statements, and rulemaking initiatives related to algorithmic systems from 2023 to 2025.

## Overview

The Federal Trade Commission has emerged as the most active federal agency on algorithmic accountability, operating through a combination of enforcement actions, consent orders, policy statements, and pre-rulemaking activity. This report synthesizes two years of FTC activity in this domain and assesses its cumulative effect on the field.

The picture that emerges is of an agency with serious ambitions operating under significant legal and resource constraints — one whose influence on industry practice has been disproportionate to the formal regulatory authority it has exercised.

Digital Markets Act at One Year: Lessons for U.S. Competition Policy
Evaluates the DMA's first year of enforcement and draws concrete lessons for U.S. policymakers considering analogous legislation.

## Overview

The European Union's Digital Markets Act entered into force in May 2023 and began applying to designated "gatekeepers" in March 2024. One year into active enforcement, enough has occurred — designations, non-compliance proceedings, interoperability disputes — to draw preliminary lessons for policymakers elsewhere, particularly in the United States.

This report evaluates the DMA's first enforcement year and identifies the provisions, approaches, and institutional arrangements most relevant to ongoing U.S. competition policy debates.

California's AB 1008 and the Limits of State AI Liability Frameworks
California's latest attempt to assign civil liability for AI-caused harm raises questions about what state law can actually accomplish in this space.

California has attempted more AI legislation than any other state, and AB 1008 continues that pattern. The bill would create a civil cause of action for harm caused by AI systems that fail to meet a reasonable care standard — a straightforward-sounding proposition that turns out to be surprisingly difficult to operationalize.

## What the Bill Proposes

AB 1008 creates liability for "AI developers, deployers, and operators" who fail to exercise reasonable care in the design, deployment, or operation of AI systems that cause physical, financial, or dignitary harm. The bill includes a rebuttable presumption of unreasonable care when a defendant fails to maintain required documentation of AI system design and testing.

## The Operational Challenges

The liability chain the bill contemplates — developer to deployer to operator to end user — maps poorly onto how AI systems are actually distributed. A foundation model developer exercises little control over downstream deployment; a deployer may have limited visibility into how an enterprise customer operates the system in practice.

The reasonable care standard also interacts awkwardly with the state of AI evaluation science. What does reasonable care require in testing a model for bias, when the methods for doing so are actively contested among researchers?

## What State Law Can and Cannot Do

State tort law can create incentives for care. It cannot establish the kind of prospective technical standards that would give developers clear guidance on what compliance looks like. For that, federal agency rulemaking — with the technical capacity and national scope that entails — is more appropriate.

AB 1008 may nonetheless be useful as a gap-filler in the absence of federal action, even if its practical effect is modest.

After Loper Bright: How Agencies Are Adjusting Their Rulemaking Strategies
Six months after the Court's Chevron overruling, a look at how the FTC, FCC, and CFPB are adapting their approach to technology-related rulemaking.

*Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo* overruled *Chevron* in June 2024. Six months later, the practical effects on technology regulation are becoming visible — not in dramatic reversals, but in the quieter shift in how agencies are writing rules and litigating their authority.

## The Changed Incentive Structure

Under Chevron, agencies could adopt reasonable interpretations of ambiguous statutory text with some confidence that courts would defer to them. Under *Loper Bright*, courts must exercise independent judgment on statutory questions. This changes the calculus for agencies in two ways.

First, agencies have stronger incentives to ground rulemakings in specific statutory text rather than broad grants of authority. Vague authority claims that might have survived Chevron review are now litigation targets.

Second, agencies are investing more in the legislative record — building administrative records that document statutory basis in detail, anticipating the kind of close textual scrutiny courts will now apply.

## What We're Seeing at Each Agency

The FTC has shifted toward using its Section 5 unfair methods of competition authority, which has clearer statutory grounding than some of the broader commercial surveillance theories the Commission had been developing. The FCC's approach to broadband regulation has become notably more cautious following the *Loper Bright* decision.

The CFPB has responded by tightening the statutory citations in pending rulemakings and narrowing the scope of several proposed rules to reduce their vulnerability to challenge.

## The Technology Regulation Implications

The agencies most affected are those whose technology-related authority rests on old statutes that didn't anticipate digital markets. The FCC's Communications Act authority, the FTC's Section 5 jurisdiction, and the CFPB's Dodd-Frank grants all face new uncertainty in a post-Chevron world.

What We’re Seeing at Each Agency
The DMA's first year of enforcement offers concrete evidence for U.S. policymakers debating analogous platform legislation — some of it surprising.

One year into active enforcement of the Digital Markets Act, the European Commission has designated six gatekeepers, opened non-compliance proceedings against three, and issued its first set of interoperability mandates. The evidence base for U.S. policymakers is now substantial enough to draw preliminary lessons.

## What Has Worked

The gatekeeper designation process moved faster than critics predicted. The Commission met its statutory deadlines and the designated gatekeepers — Apple, Alphabet, Meta, Amazon, Microsoft, and ByteDance — have largely complied with the designation process itself, reserving their legal challenges for specific obligations rather than the framework as a whole.

Interoperability mandates for messaging services have produced measurable results. Meta's compliance with the requirement to open WhatsApp to third-party messaging clients was slower than required, but the technical infrastructure is now in place.

## What Has Not Worked as Expected

The behavioral remedies around self-preferencing have proven harder to enforce than the framework anticipated. Demonstrating that a gatekeeper's algorithm systematically favors its own services requires sustained technical analysis that the Commission is still developing capacity to conduct.

The consent requirement for data combination across services has produced a proliferation of consent dialogs that most users dismiss without reading — a compliance-without-effect problem the DMA did not anticipate.

## Lessons for U.S. Policymakers

American proposals for platform regulation tend to focus on prohibition and divestiture rather than behavioral obligation. The DMA's experience suggests that behavioral obligations can produce real market effects, but require regulatory capacity and technical expertise that most U.S. agencies currently lack.

The Executive Order on AI and Federal Procurement: What It Actually Requires
A close read of the latest executive action on AI in federal contracting, separating the binding requirements from the aspirational language.

Executive orders on technology tend to arrive with sweeping language and modest operative effect. The latest order on AI in federal procurement is no exception — but buried within its aspirational framing are specific requirements that federal contractors and agency procurement officers should read carefully.

## What the Order Actually Mandates

The order's binding provisions cluster around three areas: pre-deployment review requirements for AI systems used in high-stakes federal decisions, documentation and audit obligations for existing deployments, and new contractor disclosure requirements for AI components in federal contracts.

The pre-deployment review provisions are the most significant. Agencies deploying AI systems in benefits determination, enforcement, or public safety contexts must now complete a structured impact assessment before go-live. The order specifies minimum elements of that assessment but leaves methodology to agency discretion — a gap that OMB guidance will need to fill.

## What It Doesn't Do

The order does not establish a centralized federal AI registry, create a new enforcement mechanism, or impose liability for AI-caused harm in federal programs. Several draft provisions that would have done these things were removed before publication.

The contractor disclosure requirements are narrower than early drafts suggested. They apply to contracts above a specified dollar threshold and cover AI components that "substantially influence" contract deliverables — a term the order does not define.

## Implications for Contractors

Federal contractors with AI components in their products or workflows should audit their existing contracts against the new disclosure threshold, document AI system characteristics for any federal engagement, and flag the pre-deployment review requirement for new bids in covered domains.

The contractor disclosure requirements are narrower than early drafts suggested. They apply to contracts above a specified dollar threshold and cover AI components that “substantially influence” contract deliverables — a term the order does not define.

What the DOJ's Google Remedies Brief Actually Proposes
A plain-language breakdown of the Justice Department's proposed structural and behavioral remedies in the Google search monopolization case.

The Department of Justice's remedies brief in *United States v. Google* runs to hundreds of pages and has generated considerable coverage. Most of that coverage has focused on the most dramatic proposals — divestiture of Chrome, forced syndication of search data — while the full range of proposed remedies has received less attention.

This article walks through the brief's major proposals in plain language.

## Structural Remedies

The DOJ's primary structural ask is divestiture of the Chrome browser. The theory is straightforward: Chrome's market position as the dominant browser creates a distribution advantage for Google Search that compounds the exclusionary effects found at trial. Requiring Google to divest Chrome would, in theory, create a competitively neutral browser market.

The brief also proposes requiring Google to divest its interests in AI training data pipelines that depend on search query data — a forward-looking remedy aimed at preventing the search monopoly from bootstrapping an AI data advantage.

## Behavioral Remedies

The behavioral proposals are more detailed and arguably more immediately consequential. They include prohibition on paying for default search placement (the conduct at the center of the liability finding), mandatory syndication of search index data to qualifying competitors on non-discriminatory terms, and interoperability requirements for search advertising tools.

## What to Watch

The structural remedies face the highest litigation risk — divestiture remedies in monopolization cases are rare and courts apply heightened scrutiny. The behavioral remedies are more likely to survive and, if properly designed, more likely to produce durable competitive effects.
